Title: The Power Broker
Author: Robert Caro
Notes: The definitive account of how Robert Moses shaped New York — and a masterclass in how infrastructure decisions are really political decisions.

Title: Electricity Act 2003
Author: Parliament of India
Notes: The statute everything in Indian power sector law flows from. Worth re-reading when regulatory arguments get creative.

Title: The High Cost of Free Parking
Author: Donald Shoup
Notes: Shoup's argument that minimum parking requirements distort cities more than most planners admit. Slow going but the core thesis is airtight.

Title: Energy Watchdog v. CERC
Author: Supreme Court of India
Notes: Foundational on force majeure and change-in-law under long-term PPAs. Cited constantly in coal cost pass-through disputes.

Title: Power System Economics
Author: Steven Stoft
Notes: The clearest first-principles treatment of why electricity markets are weird. The short-run profit function framing has stayed with me.

Title: Energy and Civilization: A History
Author: Vaclav Smil
Notes: The book to read before forming any strong opinion about the energy transition. Smil is allergic to wishful thinking.

Title: The Grid
Author: Gretchen Bakke
Notes: Less technical than Stoft but vivid on the political and physical reality of the American grid.

Title: Gujarat Urja Vikas Nigam Ltd. v. Adani Power
Author: Supreme Court of India
Notes: Important sequel to Energy Watchdog on what counts as a change-in-law event.

Title: World Energy Outlook 2024
Author: International Energy Agency
Notes: Read selectively with the methodology annexes. Useful as common reference points in client conversations.

Title: The Economics of Renewable Energy Support Schemes
Author: D. Newbery
Notes: Crisp comparison of feed-in tariffs CfDs and quota systems. Worth a re-read whenever a new subsidy design lands.

Title: Thinking Fast and Slow
Author: Daniel Kahneman
Notes: The canonical account of dual-process theory. Most useful for understanding why clients hold positions they cannot fully defend.

Title: The Checklist Manifesto
Author: Atul Gawande
Notes: Written for surgeons but the argument transfers cleanly to complex transactional work. Errors of ineptitude not ignorance.

Title: Why Nations Fail
Author: Daron Acemoglu and James Robinson
Notes: Institutions not geography or culture determine long-run prosperity. A useful frame for thinking about why infrastructure investment sticks in some countries and not others.

Title: The Entrepreneurial State
Author: Mariana Mazzucato
Notes: A corrective to the private-sector-leads-innovation story. Particularly useful when thinking about the role of public finance in energy transitions.

Title: Concession Agreements: A Practitioner's Guide
Author: Delmon
Notes: Dense but the most useful single reference for structuring project agreements in developing markets.

Title: Public Private Partnerships in Infrastructure
Author: World Bank Group
Notes: The Bank's operational framework document. Useful to have read before working on any multilateral-funded project.

Title: Infrastructure as an Asset Class
Author: Weber and Alfen
Notes: A good primer on how institutional investors think about infrastructure. Helpful context when advising on fund-side transactions.

Title: Contract Theory
Author: Bolton and Dewatripont
Notes: The economics behind why contracts are structured the way they are. Slow going but changes how you read a term sheet.

Title: The Nature of the Firm
Author: Ronald Coase
Notes: Four pages that explain more about why organisations exist than most textbooks manage in four hundred. Still the clearest statement of transaction cost economics.

Title: Arbitration in India
Author: Bhat
Notes: The most useful domestic reference for institutional arbitration practice. Kept close when drafting dispute resolution clauses.
